channel.title: Ajahn Funn Ācāro Quotes
channel.description: Dhamma quotes by Ajahn Funn Ācāro, updated every 24 hours

item.title: Ajahn Funn: If our mind is calm and still, not reaching out forward or backward, left or right, above or below, but established right in…
item.description:
Establishing the Knower Within: Suffering Is Because of Delusion

If our mind is calm and still, not reaching out forward or backward, left or right, above or below, but established right in the middle of knowing — not conceiving of past or future, just determining and watching the present moment — sitting and watching right now: is it happy or suffering? Suffering is something we call suffering; happiness is something we call happiness. If we do not call it happiness, will it be there? We grasp at happiness, so it becomes happiness. We grasp at suffering, so it becomes suffering. Therefore, we must remain calm and still, not leaning here or there. Establish yourself in buddho — the knower within.

All of us wish to be free from suffering. Where is suffering? Suffering is not in trees, mountains, or jungles. This very heart of ours is suffering. Suffering because of delusion. Now, as for freedom from suffering: as we sit here, our mind is refreshed. Buddho — establishing the knower within — it does not suffer. When the mind does not suffer, that means it is free from suffering. Happiness because there is no suffering. This is nothing else.

Suffering is not because of clothes, not because of a piece of cloth, not because of a dwelling, not because of money or wealth, not because of relatives or siblings. Is it that parents and siblings cause suffering? That possessions and money cause suffering? That work causes suffering? Look: as we sit here, what is there? Sitting here right now, there is absolutely nothing, isn't that true? Where is any business? Where is any work? If we just sit still, why would there be suffering? That's the point: suffering is because our mind is deluded.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: When we know and see like this, we withdraw clinging to the aggregates. It is not a self, not a being, not a…
item.description:
When We See the Truth, We Withdraw Clinging to Aggregates and Let Go

When we know and see like this, we withdraw clinging to the aggregates. It is not a self, not a being, not a person, not beautiful, not handsome. Our mind can then let go. It abandons personality view. No matter what anyone says or curses, we don’t get angry. There’s nothing – why get angry? So the Buddha taught this to enable us to abandon greed, hatred, and delusion. By contemplating this mūla-kammaṭṭhāna, when we see the truth, we withdraw from becomings and births; we no longer wander on in birth and death. If we see this, we know Dhamma. When we know Dhamma and see Dhamma, our mind becomes steady and unshakable.

From now on, you must go to the temple every day to listen to Dhamma. Remember this. Our refuge is Buddho – the Buddha; Dhammo – the Dhamma; Saṅgho – the Noble Sangha. You must pay homage every day. Our refuges are only these – nothing else can be relied upon. Material possessions, money, wealth, buildings, houses – they cannot be relied upon. Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho – when condensed, become the single word Buddho. If you lack Buddha – that is, the knowing one – you cannot rely on anything at all. You cannot rely on Dhammo nor Saṅgho.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Now, sit down. Sit in meditation. Sit comfortably. Arrange your posture and demeanor with dignity, with a bright, smiling face. When you have…
item.description:
Meditating on Buddho, Buddho – Watching the Knower in the Heart

Now, sit down. Sit in meditation. Sit comfortably. Arrange your posture and demeanor with dignity, with a bright, smiling face. When you have made the body comfortable, recollect our refuge. What is our refuge? The Buddha, the Dhamma, the Saṅgha – these are our refuge. Recollect the Buddha in your mind, the Dhamma in your mind, the noble Saṅgha disciples in your mind. Have confident faith in that way. Then recite the meditation phrase: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho – three times. Then consolidate it into just Buddho, Buddho – one word. Close your eyes, close your mouth. Recollect it inwardly. Don't wiggle your tongue. We want to know what is lacking in ourselves – what is good, what is not good, so that we know. We want to know whether it is good or bad. What level are we on? In what realm? Why have we done something and not achieved our aspiration? For what reason? Look and see. How will we know? Recollect Buddho, Buddho – wherever awareness arises, set up mindfulness and look intently at that knowing. Let the ear listen there as well; let everything look there. Don't look elsewhere.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This knowing is extremely important. We must develop it further, make it further, contemplate it further, determine it further. You must focus on…
item.description:
Whatever You Grasp, It Becomes That – The Teacher Taught Not to Grasp Anything

This knowing is extremely important. We must develop it further, make it further, contemplate it further, determine it further. You must focus on one thing only. When we determine the mind to be still on one thing, then all diseases are calmed and cease completely. Dangers cease completely. The defilements, craving, lust, greed, hatred, delusion cease by themselves. Because that mind is no longer anything – then who would become anything? Dangers and enmity cease by themselves. That one has no danger – where would danger come from? That one has no enmity – where would enmity come from? That one has no kamma – where would kamma come from? That one has no evil – where would evil come from? That one has no suffering – where would suffering come from? It is merely the knower. Make it know right now. See clearly, directly, with certainty. Knowing is not suffering, not danger, not enmity. Right now, if we grasp danger, it becomes danger. If we grasp enmity, it becomes enmity. If we grasp kamma, it becomes kamma. If we grasp suffering, it becomes suffering. Whatever you grasp, it becomes that.

Therefore, the Teacher taught not to grasp anything at all. He taught to release, to let go, to abandon. In the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, he said: "Cāgo paṭinissaggo mutti anālayo." Cāgo means relinquishment, or abandoning, letting go, releasing.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Relying on this or that is useless. Possessions, money, gold, buildings, houses – you can’t rely on them. Father, mother, grandparents – you…
item.description:
Without the Buddha (Knowing), You Cannot Rely on Anything – Not Even Your Own Body

Relying on this or that is useless. Possessions, money, gold, buildings, houses – you can’t rely on them. Father, mother, grandparents – you can’t rely on them. Rice, water, food – you can’t rely on them. Not to mention the eyes, ears, nose – even our own body we cannot rely on. Without the Buddha alone, you can’t rely on anything. Without “Buddho” – the Buddha alone – what can you rely on? Why can’t you rely? Without “Buddha” you can’t rely. Buddha means “knowing.” Without knowledge, what can you rely on? If we can do something, then we can rely on it. If we don’t practice, we can’t rely on it. If we want something, we don’t get it. Why can’t we rely on it? Because we haven’t practiced it. Buddha is knowledge. Without knowledge, you can’t rely. It’s like a dead person. Have you ever seen a dead person? A dead person can’t do anything; you can’t rely on them because they have no Buddha. Understand this point: without the Buddha alone – even though you have eyes, ears, a mouth – the ears are just like pot ears, the mouth like a basket mouth. So what? What can you do? This is Buddho. Do you remember?

item.title: Ajahn Funn: How did the Buddha become enlightened? He investigated paṭiccasamuppāda – this very thing. He investigated birth, aging, sickness, death – these very four…
item.description:
How the Buddha Became Enlightened – Investigating Birth, Aging, Sickness, Death

How did the Buddha become enlightened? He investigated paṭiccasamuppāda – this very thing. He investigated birth, aging, sickness, death – these very four noble truths. Thus he saw birth, aging, and death as noble truths. He didn't contemplate anything else. Now we have also investigated deeply. These four elements – earth, water, fire, air – come together to form the body. We separate them out into water element, earth element, fire element. Then we penetrate to the knower. We know them as elements, see them as elements. The knower doesn't become anything. Then there is no grasping or clinging. One is released and freed. That's how it is. Therefore, investigate deeply until you reach the knower, and you will see these truths for yourselves.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Everyone has heard. Next: sanditṭhiko — the practitioner knows for themselves, sees for themselves. If I were to explain there would be no…
item.description:
Paṭipatti-Pūjā: The Supreme Offering – No Other Way to Cleanse Kamma

Everyone has heard. Next: sanditṭhiko — the practitioner knows for themselves, sees for themselves. If I were to explain there would be no end; I've already explained. Everyone has heard. Opanayiko — leading inward to oneself. Paccattaṃ — known individually. If one does not know oneself, no matter how much others tell you, you will not believe.

Happiness and suffering, good and evil — when one sees it oneself, one believes. This, remember. The merit we are making here is called paṭipatti-pūjā — the supreme, most excellent offering. We are making merit that cleanses away demerit and kamma. There is no other way to cleanse it. Washing with water cannot cleanse it; cutting with a knife or sword cannot do it. It is by sitting in samādhi meditation — when our mind is calm, then kamma ends. This person does not create kamma. Where would kamma come from? So let us all know this. From now on, make the greatest merit, called paṭipatti-pūjā, the supreme, most excellent offering. From now on, remember: the practitioner sees for themselves, knows for themselves. If they do not know, others cannot tell them. I have explained the method of practice. Note it well: whatever sounds you hear, know that those things are not dangerous and we need not be disturbed.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: In the past, people worried about this and that, couldn't go anywhere, only clung and grasped. Afraid of lacking, afraid of hunger, afraid…
item.description:
The Dead Don't Complain Even When Fire Is Piled On Them

In the past, people worried about this and that, couldn't go anywhere, only clung and grasped. Afraid of lacking, afraid of hunger, afraid of suffering, afraid of poverty. Now, they are not afraid of anything at all. Even when fire is piled on them, they don't cry out "Ouch!" even once. We should contemplate to know and see like that, so we won't be deluded. This is the Dhamma teaching. The living don't listen. When performing bang-sukun, they say they are "pulling" for the dead to go to heaven or nibbāna. Hey, how would they know?

When a person is about to die, coldness rises from the feet, from the arms and hands, while the head grows cool. They all converge at the heart. If that person has merit, has built merit, the merit goes to produce a new birth. Why would it stay? Why would it stay in that coffin? If it were still there, it would be wriggling around, wouldn't it? It would be able to speak. Why would it lie still, continuing on in becoming? If there is demerit, it experiences suffering. When the khandhas are about to cease, it begins. Bhava, bhave sambhavanti – it is restless because it has no place to cling to, no place to go. Once it sees, in a flash it's gone, like taking a photograph. How could it remain? It's not that performing bang-sukun sends that person to heaven or nibbāna. It is performed for the living to see. The teachers want us to see, to contemplate: after all that clinging to this and that, what did we gain? What is there?

item.title: Ajahn Funn: See? Is our mind at ease? Sitting is comfortable. Kāya-lahutā (lightness of body), citta-lahutā (lightness of mind) — light in body and self,…
item.description:
Kāya-Lahutā, Citta-Lahutā: Lightness of Body and Mind – Diseases Dissolve

See? Is our mind at ease? Sitting is comfortable. Kāya-lahutā (lightness of body), citta-lahutā (lightness of mind) — light in body and self, light in form and physique. Diseases and dangers disappear, calamities and misfortunes disappear, defilements and nuisances disappear, evil and depravity disappear, misery and poverty disappear. Because our mind is not poor. Our mind has happiness, our mind has ease — not because of material goods or money, not because of a comfortable house or work. Our mind is at ease.

When our mind is at ease, then work is easy, study is easy, earning a living is easy, family is easy. When it is like that, people in the marketplace are at ease, the country is at ease. When we pass away (extinguish the aggregates), we dwell in ease. We do not need to think about past or future. The past is gone — good or bad has already happened. The future has not yet arrived. When the present is at ease, the future is at ease because this person is one who makes merit. This is called merit. Know and understand this. Virtue and goodness — know it.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: We must have virtue (sīla) and Dhamma. Sīla is the normalcy of body and mind. Sīla is a cool, calm, happy, at-ease heart…
item.description:
Sīla-Dhamma: Normalcy of Body and Mind – Calm, Happy, at Ease

We must have virtue (sīla) and Dhamma. Sīla is the normalcy of body and mind. Sīla is a cool, calm, happy, at-ease heart – that is called sīla-dhamma. If the mind is chaotic and distressed, it is not sīla nor Dhamma. A troubled, difficult mind is not sīla nor Dhamma. A body that is orderly and a mind that is orderly – that is sīla. Normal body, normal mind – that is the matter of sīla. Having happiness and comfort – that is the matter of sīla. An uncalm, chaotic, distressed mind is not sīla.

Therefore, the Buddha suppressed distress. That's how it is. Where does he suppress it? With what? Distress is burning with the fire of lust (rāga), burning with the fire of greed (lobha), burning with the fire of hatred (dosa). Ādittaṁ jātiyā jarā maraṇena – burning with birth, burning with aging, burning with sickness, burning with death. Now, if we suppress all suffering, then lust, greed, and hatred also cease. Our mind does not arise, does not age, and so it is not distressed. Whatever does not arise does not burn. When there is no more birth, there is no aging – so it does not burn.
